nativeRange.habitat: Pools, runs, and backwaters of the Rio Grande and associated tributaries; also Río San Juan (Tamaulipas, Mexico). Prefers slow-moving to moderately flowing warm rivers and lakes with rocky or sandy substrates; occurs in areas with submerged vegetation and rocky cover.

decorPreferences: large flat rocks or slate for spawning sites
heavy smooth substrate (coarse sand or fine gravel) — will excavate extensively
minimal or no live plants (will uproot); plastic or robust potted plants only
large cave or ledge structures for shelter and spawning

diet.notes: Broad omnivore in the wild, consuming invertebrates, small fish, plant material, and detritus. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality large pellets or sticks as a staple; supplement with earthworms, large frozen foods (krill, prawn, mussel), and occasional blanched vegetables. Avoid feeder goldfish. Feed once or twice daily in quantities consumed within a few minutes.

goodWith: bonded same-species pair (only during successful conditioning)
very large robust cichlids of comparable size in very large tanks (Oscar, Vieja, Dovii — extreme caution)
large armored catfish (Pterygoplichthys) as dither/bottom occupant
avoidWith: all smaller fish (will be eaten or harassed to death)
most other cichlids unless enormous tank with visual breaks

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Herichthys cyanoguttatus showing adult male coloration with blue-green iridescent spotting.
Wikimedia Commons image of adult Texas Cichlid; good color representation of the iridescent spotting.

summary: The only cichlid native to the United States, the Texas Cichlid (Rio Grande Cichlid) reaches 30 cm and is highly aggressive. Requires a specialist setup of 500 L+, kept as a lone specimen or bonded pair only. Cool-tolerant down to 15°C. Contributed to the Flowerhorn hybrid lineage.
careNotes: Herichthys cyanoguttatus is the only cichlid species native to the United States, occurring naturally in the Rio Grande drainage of Texas and adjacent Mexico — a distinction that makes it notable among North American ichthyofauna. Despite its native status, it is a demanding and highly aggressive species that is unsuitable for community aquariums or novice keepers. Adults can reach 30 cm and require at minimum a 500 L tank with a footprint no shorter than 180 cm to allow adequate territory. Because of extreme intraspecific and interspecific aggression, the Texas Cichlid is almost always kept either as a single specimen or as a carefully bonded pair. Mixing with other tank inhabitants is extremely risky; only very large, robust cichlids in enormous setups should be attempted, and even then casualties are common. The species is a vigorous substrate excavator and will rearrange any decor not firmly weighted down. Plants will be uprooted; only robust potted or artificial vegetation is practical. Water quality must be maintained diligently — large regular water changes (30–40% weekly) are advised given the bioload a large cichlid produces. Unlike many tropical cichlids, H. cyanoguttatus is notably cool-tolerant, surviving temperatures down to 15°C, which reflects its semi-subtropical native range. Maintain pH 6.5–8.0 and moderate hardness. The species contributed to the development of "Green Texas" cichlid selectively bred lines and has been used in hybridization that eventually produced Flowerhorn hybrids — a piece of aquarium history that has fueled its collector appeal. A nuchal hump develops on dominant males. The iridescent blue-green pearl spotting over the body and head (particularly prominent on mature males) is the source of both its scientific name (cyanoguttatus = blue-spotted) and common name Pearl Cichlid.
breedingNotes: Texas Cichlids are substrate spawners that form strong monogamous pair bonds. Conditioning a compatible pair can be challenging — introductions should be made carefully, ideally by growing juveniles together or by introducing a smaller female to a divided tank and allowing gradual bonding. Spawning occurs on large flat rocks, slate, or excavated substrate pits cleaned by the pair. Females deposit up to 1,000 adhesive eggs; both parents guard the clutch intensely and will attack anything that approaches, including the aquarist’s hand. Fry are moved by parents to pre-excavated pits before and after hatching. Parental defense is extreme and lasts for several weeks. Pair bonds can break down outside spawning conditions, leading to severe aggression toward the female; a divider should always be on hand. Water temperature at the warmer end of tolerance (24–26°C) promotes spawning readiness.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Herichthys cyanoguttatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 30 cm), temperature range (15–28°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (5–20 dH), native range (Rio Grande, Tamaulipas), diet (omnivore), breeding behavior (substrate spawner, biparental care), and aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Herichthys cyanoguttatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 30 cm TL), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Rio Grande Texas/Mexico), diet, and cool-tolerance data. Confirmed as only US-native cichlid.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Texas Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-perspective care guide. Confirmed 500 L+ tank requirement, aggressive temperament requiring species-only or bonded-pair setup, omnivore diet, substrate excavation behavior, and cool-tolerance. Supplemented tank decor and compatibility notes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Texas Cichlid (Herichthys cyanoguttatus)
additional.notes: Supplemented information on Flowerhorn lineage contributions, “Green Texas” selective-bred strain history, and specialist hobbyist notes on extreme parental defense behavior.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Herichthys cyanoguttatus species profile
additional.notes: Additional confirmation of tank size recommendations, bonded-pair husbandry approach, nuchal hump development in males, and breeding pit excavation behavior.

fish.breedingNotes: Substrate spawner; forms strong monogamous pair bond. Flat rock or substrate pit spawning site; up to ~1,000 adhesive eggs per spawn. Both parents guard clutch and fry with extreme aggression. Pair conditioning requires care — keep divider available to prevent female fatalities if pair bond breaks down.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 30cm; extremely aggressive. Single-fish species-only tank. Requires 500L+. ONLY native US cichlid (Rio Grande).
